one_line_definition: Manufacturing processes that remove material from a workpiece using cutting tools to achieve desired shape and surface finish
Materials used to make cutting tools, chosen for hardness and wear resistance at cutting temperatures (high-speed steel, cemented carbide, ceramics, cubic boron nitride, diamond)
A measure of how easily a material can be cut, typically referenced to a standard material (e.g., AISI 1112 steel = 100%)

aliases_seen: subsurface damage; residual stress from machining
one_line_definition: The totality of surface and subsurface properties (microstructure, hardness, residual stress) induced by a machining process, distinct from geometric surface roughness
Empirical equation relating cutting speed V and tool life T, with material constants n and C, used to optimise productivity vs. tool cost

aliases_seen: EDM; ECM; laser machining; ultrasonic machining
one_line_definition: Material removal processes that do not rely on mechanical cutting action, including electrical discharge, electrochemical, laser, and ultrasonic methods
A control system integrated into the device it controls, with dedicated hardware and software optimised for a specific real-time control function
A control system that must complete specified computations within strict timing constraints, failing if deadlines are missed
A control architecture where control functions are distributed across multiple networked nodes rather than centralised in a single controller
An industrial digital computer adapted for control of manufacturing processes, with rugged hardware and scan-cycle programming model
A feedback control algorithm that computes a control output as a weighted sum of the error, its integral, and its derivative
A class of industrial communication networks connecting field devices (sensors, actuators) to controllers using digital serial communication
A supervisory-level control system architecture for monitoring and controlling distributed industrial processes over wide areas
Hardware and software that allows human operators to monitor and interact with industrial control systems
A reconfigurable integrated circuit that can implement custom digital logic; widely used in industrial control for high-speed parallel processing
A motor with integrated feedback control for precise position, velocity, and torque control; preferred above ~1000 rpm and 200 W
A brushless DC motor that divides a full rotation into discrete steps; preferred for low-speed, lower-power applications not requiring closed-loop feedback
The tendency of an aircraft to return to its trimmed angle of attack after a pitch perturbation; requires ∂Mcg/∂α < 0
The point on an aircraft about which the pitching moment is independent of angle of attack; its position relative to the CG determines static margin
Non-dimensional distance between centre of gravity and neutral point, measuring the degree of longitudinal static stability
The aerodynamic moment about a control surface hinge line; determines the stick force a pilot must exert to deflect the control surface
A lightly damped, long-period oscillation in aircraft speed and altitude at approximately constant angle of attack, driven by exchange of kinetic and potential energy
A small auxiliary control surface that adjusts the hinge moment of a primary control surface without significantly changing its lift, used to reduce pilot stick forces
A nose-down pitch change at high subsonic Mach numbers caused by rearward shift of the aerodynamic centre as compressibility effects move pressure distributions aft
The response of an aircraft over time following a perturbation; includes short-period, phugoid, Dutch roll, spiral, and roll modes
The arithmetic mean of absolute deviations of the surface height profile from its mean line, the most widely standardised roughness parameter
Square root of the mean squared deviation of surface heights from the mean line; standard deviation of height distribution; σ ≈ 1.25 Ra for Gaussian surfaces
Third standardised central moment of the surface height distribution; negative for surfaces with deep valleys, positive for surfaces with high peaks
Fourth standardised central moment of surface heights; K = 3 for Gaussian; K > 3 (leptokurtic) indicates occasional very high asperities
A thin layer of microcrystalline or amorphous material formed on a solid surface during machining or forming, with different properties from the bulk
Description of surface topography using fractal (scale-invariant) statistics; provides scale-independent roughness descriptors unlike Ra or Rq
Optical technique measuring surface height variations by analysing interference fringe phase shifts; vertical resolution < λ/100 (~5 nm for visible light)
Optical profilometry technique measuring surface height from the position of maximum fringe contrast of a white-light interferogram; avoids height ambiguity of monochromatic interferometry

aliases_seen: AD scattering; light scattering roughness measurement
one_line_definition: Optical roughness measurement based on the angular distribution of scattered laser light; limited to smooth surfaces with σ << λ (typically σ < 0.1 µm)
A local maximum in a surface height profile (peak in 2D, summit in 3D); the mechanical contact of asperities governs real contact area, friction, and wear
